summary: Gruber argues that Apple's refusal to add Flash to the iPhone is primarily a strategic business decision, not a technical limitation. He frames Flash as the 'wrong sort of proprietary' — technology controlled by a competitor (Adobe) that would undermine Apple's complete control over the iPhone software stack. Since the mobile web market is wide open and Flash isn't yet entrenched there, Apple has no incentive to help Adobe establish dominance in mobile the way it achieved on desktop. Gruber notes that Apple's entire mobile platform runs on software it fully controls, and adding Flash would introduce dependency, risk, and a muddled developer message for negligible sales benefit.
key_points: Apple views Flash as the 'wrong sort of proprietary' — controlled by a competitor rather than by Apple or open standards
The mobile web market is wide open without Flash entrenchment, giving Apple a strategic opportunity to push for a Flash-free mobile future
Apple controls the entire iPhone software stack top to bottom and has no incentive to cede any control to Adobe
The historical relationship between Apple/NeXT and Adobe has been fraught, including restrictive Display PostScript licensing terms
The upcoming iPhone SDK won't allow third parties to create system-level plugins, so Adobe can't port Flash independently
money_quotes: Could Apple do it? Yes, but it wouldn't be easy. But would they? I say no — even if it were easy.
Flash, on the other hand, is (from Apple's perspective) the wrong sort of proprietary — owned and controlled by another company.
Why would Apple help Adobe establish Flash as a de facto standard for the mobile web, too?
As it stands today, Apple is dependent on no one other than itself for the software on the iPhone. Apple controls the source code to the whole thing, from top to bottom.

If there is no Flash support in MobileSafari, and the absence of Flash does not affect demand for iPhones and iPods, then there is no possible way that Flash can turn into a pain in Apple's mobile ass.
What happens if Apple decides to switch the iPhone to a different CPU architecture and Adobe says Flash won't simply recompile?
No one is going to get to diddle with MobileSafari without Apple's approval.
key_insight: Apple's refusal to support Flash on iPhone was a calculated strategic move to prevent a competitor's proprietary technology from gaining a foothold in the wide-open mobile market, where Apple controlled the entire stack.
